segments.title: Theodore Roosevelt's blockade lesson from the War of 1812
segments.summary: Roosevelt's history of the War of 1812 turns Britain's blockade into an argument for American naval readiness and a fleet able to protect an extended coastline.
segments.body: The reading follows the Royal Navy's tightening blockade as British resources became available after Napoleon's reverses in Europe. Privateers and a few successful cruisers could win prestige, but they could not keep commerce moving or prevent attacks along the American coast. Dr. Clarke highlights Roosevelt's larger conclusion: an ocean-facing state cannot substitute isolated tactical successes for the sea power needed to repel sustained pressure.

evidence.note: The reading describes the British blockade, the limits of American privateers and cruisers, and Roosevelt's argument for a great fighting navy.

segments.title: Admiral Bacon on strategy and the peacetime arms trade
segments.summary: Admiral Reginald Bacon broadens strategy beyond military command and describes how armament firms pursued influence, markets, and foreign relationships in peacetime.
segments.body: Bacon defines strategy as planning toward a desired result, then applies the idea to business and the armaments industry. His account argues that war could bring heavy costs, taxation, new investment, and a damaging postwar slump, so firms cultivated long-term foreign relationships and reputation instead. Dr. Clarke recasts that supposedly commercial strategy as diplomacy and persistent national presence, a form of influence that modern policy debates can struggle to recognize.

evidence.note: The passage defines strategy, discusses armament manufacturers' incentives, and describes agents building foreign relationships over many years.

segments.title: Air-power theory, changing methods, and three-dimensional war
segments.summary: A Norwegian air-power study separates enduring principles from changing methods and compares the physical constraints facing armies, navies, aircraft, and submarines.
segments.body: The excerpt warns against treating the methods of one war as permanent principles, since geography and technology change the problems each service must solve. Land forces meet terrain, fleets meet coasts and rocks, and aircraft gain vertical freedom while remaining constrained by weather and fuel. Dr. Clarke adds that submarines also maneuver in three dimensions, complicating any claim that this feature belongs uniquely to air warfare.

evidence.note: The discussion distinguishes principles from methods, compares each service's movement constraints, and notes the submarine parallel to three-dimensional air warfare.

segments.title: Interwar Royal Navy planning from strategy to tactical success
segments.summary: Joseph Moretz's study of interwar capital-ship planning connects preparations against Japan and Germany with the problem of turning strategic goals into tactical success.
segments.body: The quotations stress preparing before an opponent's hostile intent becomes unmistakable and expose how plans for controlling German communications depended on Norway and France remaining secure. A 1925 naval manual insists that strategy fails if it does not provide the means for tactical success, while the Trafalgar example shows the danger of assuming equal skill in a staff appreciation. The closing framework separates policy-level strategy, theater campaigns, and battle tactics, though Dr. Clarke cautions that the operational level is rooted heavily in land-warfare thinking and does not always transfer neatly to the sea.

evidence.note: The book excerpts cover advance preparation, assumptions about Germany and Japan, tactical means, staff appreciation, and the strategy-operational-tactics hierarchy.
